title: Evaluation of the impact of sugarcane trash in situ incorporation on soil health in North Haryana.
abstract: India produces an estimated 6.38 million tons of surplus sugarcane trash annually. When burned in fields, this trash emits approximately 12,948 kg CO<sub>2</sub> equivalent greenhouse gases per hectare and causes nutrient losses (41 kg ha<sup>-1</sup> nitrogen, 5.4 kg ha<sup>-1</sup> phosphorus, and 31 kg ha<sup>-1</sup> potassium), contributing to soil degradation and air pollution. This study compares the effects of different trash management practices, sugarcane trash removal (STR), sugarcane trash burning (STB), and sugarcane trash in situ incorporation (STIC), on soil health and crop yield across the Kurukshetra, Karnal, and Yamunanagar districts of North Haryana over two cropping seasons (2020-2021 and 2021-2022). In the STIC treatment, trash was incorporated at 4.8-5.2 t ha<sup>-1</sup> using a rotavator. STIC significantly improved soil moisture retention (8.4-12.3% higher than STR and STB), microbial biomass (501.22 µg g<sup>-1</sup>), and available nitrogen (321.06 kg/ha) while reducing salinity and maintaining favorable pH. STB resulted in the highest electrical conductivity (254.95 µS cm<sup>-1</sup>) and nitrogen losses of up to 40%, underscoring its negative impacts. Principal component analysis identified organic carbon, nitrogen mineralization, and EC as key drivers of soil health. STIC treatment also achieved the highest yields (128.3-129.0 t ha<sup>-1</sup>). While policy and research in the Indo-Gangetic Plains have focused predominantly on rice and wheat residue management, the environmental burden of STB has received limited attention. This study demonstrated that STIC is a sustainable alternative to STB and is a practical approach for improving yields, restoring soil health, and mitigating pollution. The findings support scaling up the STIC through mechanization subsidies, targeted extension services, and region-specific adoption programs.
